text.text: ().

### Prerequisites

In this text, we will explore, among other topics, knowledge about the relationship of our species with others, as well as the expansion of the Universe.

Therefore, we suggest studying the first chapter of the *Atlas* (<htcite0>), as well as the text about humans (<htcite1>), available on History Tracers, before proceeding.

### Introduction

Since we use calendars daily, we often do not pay attention to how they were created and, above all, how they are used.

In this text, we wish to draw attention to some details that often lead people to conclusions that, although they do not represent their own realities, give the impression of being the "only" conclusions. To make this even clearer, we will begin with a reflection.

Reflection 1: The periods

Archaeological and anthropological findings indicate that we, Homo sapiens, had a common origin in Africa approximately 300,000 years ago (). Does this mean that we all use the same calendar?

Does that calendar have the same starting point? In other words, are we all in the year 300,000?

Why should we consider the emergence of our species as the starting date? Why not consider the beginning of the Universe, which occurred nearly 14 billion years ago (Figure 1) ()?
Figure 1: The universe through space-time ().

### The Closest Beginning (South of Abya Yala)

Throughout our lives, we have to deal with different periods of time. The period closest to us is that of our own life. Within it, our beginning is marked by birth, and from that day onward, we keep a count to know our age.

Now, when we live in a society, we need to look beyond ourselves and seek something that helps the entire community to organize itself.

To better understand this scenario, we will initially study cultures and empires from the south of America (*Abya Yala*), because the choices made by these peoples do not differ greatly from the choices made by other peoples.

#### The Incas

The information we have about the Inca calendar was initially recorded by Spanish chroniclers who, apparently, could not determine whether they were dealing with a solar or lunar calendar. It is very possible that, when writing their chronicles, they omitted information related to lunar cycles, producing a description more aligned with their own worldview (<htcite0>).

This difficulty in determining whether there was a solar, lunar, or lunisolar calendar was also highlighted by Elena Ortiz García when analyzing different chronicles from the Spanish colonial period (<htcite1>).

Furthermore, she presents another piece of information relevant to understanding the Incas and their calendars. According to her work, it would have been the Sapa Inca Pachacuti (**Figure 2**) who was responsible for implementing this system (<htcite2>). For this reason, when you select the Inca calendar in the **Date format** field, the years are counted from the estimated period of the beginning of his rule, in <htdate0>.
Figure 2: Statue of Pachacuti in the city of Cuzco (). Photo taken on , during a visit to the city.

source.text: Sitios del Patrimonio Mundial del Perú

text.imgdesc: Pachacuti is depicted atop a two-tiered fountain, holding a scepter in his right hand that reaches the ground. His left hand is raised as if pointing toward a path, with all fingers extended.

text.text:
The relationship between the solar calendar and the Inca Empire (*Tawantinsuyu*) is reinforced by the existence of pillars around Cusco (<htcite0>), with the beginning of the year occurring at what would correspond to the summer solstice — the longest day of the year in the Southern Hemisphere — on December 21 (<htcite1>). This date is associated with the beginning of the new annual cycle in various interpretations of historical records (<htcite2>).

This relationship can also be observed from the work of Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala (<htcite3>).
Livro 1: Página 1139 da obra de Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala.

#### A Cultural Complexity

Among the peoples who occupied the south of the American (*Abya Yala*) continent, the Incas and the *Tawantinsuyu* Empire are probably the best known. This fact leads us to a reflection:

Reflection 2: Greater and unique?

When there is a dominant empire, with ideas about how the world works that differ from those of other peoples, does that mean that only it possesses a valid understanding of the facts because it became larger?

If, by chance, your conclusion to this reflection was "yes," it is likely that throughout your life you have used information coming from a limited group of sources, which can make it difficult to see perspectives beyond those that have been presented to you.

Currently, there are billions of people in the world, with diverse cultures. Even so, culture is often still understood through ideas formulated about 250 years ago, which are not aligned with the contemporary understanding of this concept (<htcite0>).

This complexity led researchers to define the term "biocultural" to express the relationships of human beings among themselves and also with other species (<htcite1>). Using this concept, we can study other peoples from the south of the American (*Abya Yala*) continent who have calendars different from those of the Incas.

#### Biocultural Calendars

When we observe the Mapuche and Aymara peoples, we can notice that both use calendars whose starting point is the winter solstice — the shortest day of the year in the Southern Hemisphere — (<htcite2>). However, while the Mapuche celebrate the New Year, the *We tripantu*, between June 21 and 24, according to the Gregorian calendar, the Aymara celebrate the *Machaq mara* on June 21 (<htcite3>).

exercise_v2.question: Houve possibilidade de se considerar o calendário Inca um calendário solar?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Uma análise das crônicas nos leva a concluir que esta possibilidade existe, maas também é possível encontrar crônica indicando que ele seja lunar.

exercise_v2.question: Todos os povos do Sul comemoram seus calendários no solstícios de verão, 21 de dezembro?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Os Incas comemoravam o início de seu ano no solstício de verão, mas outros povos como mapuches e aymaras iniciavam seus anos durante o solstício de inverno.
